claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's get started. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're inquiring about:**

How old is your child? The eligibility rules differ based on age:
- Under 16 years old
- Between 16-19 years old
- 20 years or older

Please tell me:
1. How many children are you asking about?
2. What are their ages (and names if you'd like)?

This will help me guide you through the correct eligibility pathway.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. 

I'm asking about one child - my son Alex. He's 15 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me guide you through the eligibility assessment for your son Alex, who is 15 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, he falls into the automatic qualification age category. However, I need to check one important requirement:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Alex's work status:**

Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

In other words, does he either:
- Not have a job, OR
- Work less than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't have a job. He's still in school full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's good to know. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**

Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right, such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or other similar benefits claimed in his own name?

To put it simply: Does Alex claim any benefits himself (not benefits that you claim for him)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't claim any benefits himself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue checking the eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about apprenticeships:**

Is Alex currently enrolled in an apprenticeship program?

If yes, I'll need to know which type and in which country (England, Scotland, or Wales), as different rules apply. But since you mentioned he's in school full-time, I'm assuming he's not in an apprenticeship - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not doing an apprenticeship. He's just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to ask about your residency status:**

Do you meet the UK residency requirements? You qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, OR
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. **You have pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme AND you are:
   - Working and earning above the National Insurance threshold, OR
   - Actively looking for work (for up to 91 days), OR
   - Studying with sufficient resources, OR
   - Have sufficient resources to support yourself and your family (and not claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit), OR
   - Are a family member of an EEA or Swiss national with the right to reside
4. **You're living abroad as a Crown servant** or in certain qualifying countries

**Simply put: Do you normally live in the UK?** Or if not, do any of the other special circumstances apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK. We live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about responsibility for Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? You meet this requirement if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex**, OR
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** by paying at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care (this could include food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents - either weekly, monthly, or as lump sum payments), OR
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you? Most commonly, this would be satisfied if Alex lives with you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about other claimants:**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex? For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- A former partner

If someone else is claiming, we need to know if you've agreed with them about who should claim going forward.

What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue checking the eligibility requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Alex's living situation:**

Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?

1. **In hospital** - Is Alex currently staying in hospital?
2. **In care** - Is Alex in local authority care?
3. **Fostered** - Is Alex in a fostering arrangement?
4. **Normal living** - Is Alex living at home with you normally?

Based on what you've told me so far (that he lives at home with you and is in school), I'm assuming it's option 4 - normal living. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex is fostered - I'm fostering him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that - that's important information! Let me check the fostering rules.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about the fostering arrangement:**

Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement?

In other words:
- If the council is **NOT paying** for his accommodation or maintenance, you may be eligible to claim Child Benefit
- If the council **IS paying** for his accommodation or maintenance, you cannot claim Child Benefit

What is your situation - is the council paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't get anything from the council to foster him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! This confirms that the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 **Good News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!**

Based on the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

### **Why You're Eligible:**

✅ **Alex is 15 years old** - under the age limit  
✅ **He's not working full-time** - he's in school  
✅ **He doesn't claim benefits in his own right**  
✅ **He's not in a disqualifying apprenticeship** - just regular school  
✅ **You meet UK residency requirements** - you live in Manchester  
✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - he lives with you  
✅ **No one else is claiming for Alex**  
✅ **The local council is not paying** towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - this is the key factor that allows foster carers to claim Child Benefit

### **Next Steps - How to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply (though you can still claim)
5. **Payments are made every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can find more information and apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 15 years old, which is under 16 and falls within the automatic qualification age category; (2) He is not working 24 hours or more per week (he's in school full-time); (3) He does not claim any benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship (he's in regular school); (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex is fostered, the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the foster carer can claim Child Benefit. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, their 15-year-old foster child. The key factor in this case is that while Alex is fostered, the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which allows the foster carer to claim Child Benefit. All other standard eligibility requirements are also met.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit

Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You can claim Child Benefit for a fostered child if the council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit

Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 15 years old, which is under 16 and falls within the automatic qualification age category; (2) He is not working 24 hours or more per week (he's in school full-time); (3) He does not claim any benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship (he's in regular school); (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex is fostered, the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the foster carer can claim Child Benefit. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, their 15-year-old foster child. The key factor in this case is that while Alex is fostered, the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which allows the foster carer to claim Child Benefit. All other standard eligibility requirements are also met.
